role_summary: Architect of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee from SCLC's Atlanta basement in 1960; long-tenured NAACP organizer; the structural intelligence behind multiple civil rights organizations across five decades.

photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)

ai_reading: Among the corpus's 136 interviewees the embedding finds Baker discussed across eight separate transcripts spanning NAACP staff (Mildred Bond Roxborough, entry 90), SNCC organizers (Timothy Jenkins entry 120, Cleveland Sellers entry 22, Reginald Robinson entry 101, Lawrence Guyot entry 76, Charles McLaurin entry 17, Judy Richardson entry 69), and SCLC leadership (Joseph Echols Lowery entry 66). The breadth of the discussion across mutually-distant subgroups is the catalog's clearest pattern of corpus-wide structuring presence for any external figure tracked here: she is named more across distinct interview chapters than several figures whose cultural fame exceeds hers.
biographical_paragraph: Ella Baker is discussed in this corpus by 8 interviewees across the catalog (Mildred Bond Roxborough (entry 90), Timothy Jenkins (entry 120), Dr. Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), Reginald Robinson (entry 101), Joseph Echols Lowery (entry 66), Lawrence Guyot (entry 76), Judy Richardson (entry 69), Charles McLaurin (entry 17)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Ella Baker as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mildred Bond Roxborough
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mildred Bond Roxborough, a longtime NAACP staff member, recalls meeting Baker after Baker's own years on the NAACP staff and connects her to the start of SNCC:
interview_snippets.quote: Yes, after she worked for the NAACP, it's when I met her because she was a very forceful personality. And she became focused on our youth, if you recall, and actually was involved in the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.

interview_snippets.speaker: Joseph Echols Lowery
interview_snippets.lead_in: Joseph Echols Lowery, an SCLC co-founder, places Baker at the operational center of the new organization in its first years:
interview_snippets.quote: In those early first few years late 50s for SCLC much of the day-to-day work through the late 50s was carried by Miss Ella Baker. Ella was I guess the first real executive director full time executive director that we had.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what he wants people to know about the movement, SNCC field secretary Reginald Robinson answers by describing Baker's organizing method:
interview_snippets.quote: That we were lucky enough to have somebody like Ella Baker to present us with a very, very simple plan as taking people from where they are and bringing them up. Those that are already on top don't necessarily need to push, but they need to support those from the bottom.

interview_snippets.speaker: Timothy Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Timothy Jenkins, who led the secular-organizing camp inside SNCC, credits Baker with holding the religious and secular wings together:
interview_snippets.quote: And of course, I was in the secular side of that question. And Ella Baker was instrumental in harmonizing the two diverging interests.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons, then a Spelman student slipping into SNCC's Atlanta office against college rules, describes watching Baker operate:
interview_snippets.quote: But of course, over the years, I had the great privilege of seeing Miss Baker at work, as she, in a very gentle and unobtrusive way and her knowledge.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: Atlanta student-movement leader Lonnie C. King recalls how Baker's directness read to the male ministers around her (the transcript preserves the spoken rendering "showvinistic" for chauvinistic):
interview_snippets.quote: A lot of African American preachers are very showvinistic. And Miss Baker, from their perspective, did not know her place. And she was an outspoken person who would say what was on her mind.

sources.title: Barbara Ransby, Ella Baker and the Black Freedom Movement: A Radical Democratic Vision
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 51046011
